original_assessment.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control can be validated using Azure CLI and PowerShell commands as provided in the audit section.
- The commands specifically check the pricing tier of the Defender for SQL server resources, which can be queried via Azure APIs.
- Therefore, the control meets the criteria for an Automated approach since it involves standard Azure resource properties that can be queried without human judgment.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_sql_virtual_machine_defender_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check the following:
- Utilize Azure CLI to determine if Microsoft Defender for SQL is enabled for virtual machines.
- Use the command: `az security pricing show -n SqlServerVirtualMachines` to retrieve the pricing tier.
- Alternatively, PowerShell can be used with the command: `Get-AzSecurityPricing -Name 'SqlServerVirtualMachines' | Select-Object Name,PricingTier`.
- Validate that the `PricingTier` property is set to 'Standard'.
- The expected Pass criteria is that the 'PricingTier' is set to 'Standard', indicating that Microsoft Defender for SQL servers on machines is enabled.

Example API endpoint (for additional context): 
- `GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Security/pricings/sqlServerVirtualMachines?api-version=2021-01-01` 

Ensure to iterate over all relevant subscriptions or resource groups when executing the automation to comprehensively audit all SQL servers on virtual machines within the Azure environment.

review.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of this control as Automated is correct. The status of Microsoft Defender for SQL on virtual machines can indeed be verified using Azure CLI, PowerShell, and Azure APIs. These tools allow for programmatic querying of the Defender status, which aligns with the criteria for an automated approach.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9
- The automation details are technically accurate. The Azure CLI command `az security pricing show -n SqlServerVirtualMachines` and the PowerShell command `Get-AzSecurityPricing -Name 'SqlServerVirtualMachines' | Select-Object Name,PricingTier` are appropriate for checking the pricing tier, which indicates whether Microsoft Defender for SQL is enabled.
- The program name follows the convention: `azure_sql_virtual_machine_defender_enabled`, which is clear and descriptive.
- Minor improvement could be made by explicitly stating that the API version is up-to-date and ensuring that the iteration over all subscriptions and resource groups is explicitly mentioned in the script logic.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- It would be beneficial to include a check for API version compatibility to ensure future-proofing of the automation script.
- Consider adding error handling in the automation script to manage scenarios where the API call fails or returns unexpected results.
- Provide guidance on how to handle discrepancies or failures in the automation check, such as logging or alerting mechanisms.
- Ensure that the script iterates over all relevant subscriptions and resource groups, as this is crucial for a comprehensive audit.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-founded, and the technical details are accurate. The use of Azure CLI and PowerShell commands is appropriate for the task, and the automation approach is justified.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Keep as-is with minor documentation enhancements to include error handling and API version checks. The current approach is sound and aligns with Azure's capabilities for automated compliance checks.

input_row.title: Ensure That Microsoft Defender for SQL Servers on Machines Is Set To 'On'
input_row.description: Turning on Microsoft Defender for SQL servers on machines enables threat detection for SQL servers on machines, providing threat intelligence, anomaly detection, and behavior analytics in Microsoft Defender for Cloud.
input_row.rationale: Enabling Microsoft Defender for SQL servers on machines allows for greater defense- in-depth, functionality for discovering and classifying sensitive data, surfacing and mitigating potential database vulnerabilities, and detecting anomalous activities that could indicate a threat to your database. Impact: Turning on Microsoft Defender for SQL servers on machines incurs an additional cost per resource.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Microsoft Defender for Cloud. 2. Under Management, select Environment Settings. 3. Click on the subscription name. 4. Select the Defender plans blade. 5. Click Select types > in the row for Databases. 6. Ensure the toggle switch next to SQL servers on machines is set to On. Audit from Azure CLI Ensure Defender for SQL is licensed with the following command: az security pricing show -n SqlServerVirtualMachines Ensure the 'PricingTier' is set to 'Standard' Audit from PowerShell Run the following command: Get-AzSecurityPricing -Name 'SqlServerVirtualMachines' | Select-Object Name,PricingTier Ensure the 'PricingTier' is set to 'Standard' Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 6581d072-105e-4418-827f-bd446d56421b - Name: 'Azure Defender for SQL servers on machines should be enabled' • Policy ID: abfb4388-5bf4-4ad7-ba82-2cd2f41ceae9 - Name: 'Azure Defender for SQL should be enabled for unprotected Azure SQL servers'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to Microsoft Defender for Cloud. 2. Under Management, select Environment Settings. 3. Click on the subscription name. 4. Select the Defender plans blade. 5. Click Select types > in the row for Databases. 6. Set the toggle switch next to SQL servers on machines to On. 7. Select Continue. 8. Select Save. Remediate from Azure CLI Run the following command: az security pricing create -n SqlServerVirtualMachines --tier 'standard' Remediate from PowerShell Run the following command: Set-AzSecurityPricing -Name 'SqlServerVirtualMachines' -PricingTier 'Standard' Default Value: By default, Microsoft Defender plan is off. References: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security-center/defender-for-sql-usage 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security-center/security-center-detection- capabilities 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/securitycenter/pricings/update 4. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/az.security/get- azsecuritypricing 5. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-data- protection#dp-2-monitor-anomalies-and-threats-targeting-sensitive-data 6. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-logging-threat- detection#lt-1-enable-threat-detection-capabilities
input_row.impact: Turning on Microsoft Defender for SQL servers on machines incurs an additional cost per resource.
